7 min read
Just now

--

Everyone talks about queues and linked lists. Nobody talks about the mechanism that actually brings your DRAM out of reset.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I have spent a good part of my career writing firmware for DDR and LPDDR memory subsystems. If you asked me which communication mechanism I touch most often, it is not a linked list. It is not even a queue. It is a mailbox.
And yet, when I interview embedded engineers, most of them can reverse a linked list on a whiteboard but have never used a mailbox against real hardware. This article is my attempt to fix that, in plain language.
What is a mailbox, really?
Forget the textbook definitions for a second. Think of an actual mailbox outside a house.
It holds exactly one letter. The postman drops a letter in and raises the little flag. You see the flag, take the letter out, and put the flag down. Now the postman knows he can deliver the next one.
A software mailbox is exactly this:

• A small, fixed piece of memory that holds one message.

• A flag that says “message waiting” or “empty”.
One side writes the message and raises the flag. The other side sees the flag, reads the message, and…
